title: Designing a Basic Loop
concept: Wiring an LLM in a loop: prompt, parse output action, execute action, feed result back to prompt.

exercises.prompt: Quick warm-up! What makes an AI 'agent' different from a single chatbot reply?

options.text: An agent can take actions and keep working toward a goal, not just answer once.
An agent is just a chatbot with a nicer font.
An agent can only reply with one sentence at a time.

exercises.explain: An agent doesn't stop after a single answer. It can decide to do something, see what happens, and keep going until the goal is met. That 'keep going' idea is exactly what we build this lesson.

cards.title: One Reply Isn't Enough
cards.body: Ask an LLM a question and it answers once, then stops. But real tasks take many steps. What if the AI could keep going on its own?

cards.title: The Agentic Loop
cards.body: An agentic loop wires an LLM to repeat four steps: prompt the model, read the action it chooses, run that action, then feed the result back into the next prompt. It repeats until the goal is done.

cards.title: Like Cooking With a Recipe
cards.body: You read the next step (prompt), decide what to do (action), do it (execute), then taste and check (result). What you learn shapes your next move. Round and round until dinner is ready.

cards.title: Weather Helper in Action
cards.body: Prompt: 'What's the weather in Paris?' The LLM outputs the action 'check_weather(Paris)'. The code runs it, gets '18°C', and feeds that back. Now the LLM can answer the user.

cards.title: Myth: The LLM Does Everything
cards.body: The LLM doesn't actually run tools by itself. It only writes down which action to take. Your code reads that and does the real work, then hands the result back.

cards.title: One-Shot vs. Loop
cards.body: A single reply answers once and stops. A loop cycles through prompt, action, and result until the job is finished.

left.text: Ask once, get one answer, done.

right.text: Prompt → action → execute → feed back → repeat.

exercises.prompt: In the agentic loop, what happens right after the LLM outputs an action?

options.text: Your code parses the action and executes it, then feeds the result back.
The user has to retype the whole question.

exercises.explain: After the LLM picks an action, the code reads (parses) it, runs it, and returns the result to the model. That fresh result becomes part of the next prompt, keeping the loop moving.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The result of an executed action gets fed back into the next prompt.
exercises.explain: Yes! Feeding the result back is what makes it a loop. The model uses that new information to decide its next step instead of guessing blindly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing loop step.
exercises.sentence: After the code runs the action, it must feed the result back into the next prompt.
exercises.explain: The loop only works if you feed the result back. That gives the LLM the new information it needs to choose a smart next action.

exercises.prompt: Which line looks like an AI-generated action a loop would parse?

options.text: I think mountains are really cool and tall.
Please close the window, it's cold.
My favorite color is blue today.

exercises.explain: A loop parses a clear, structured action like search_web("tallest mountain"). The code can read that command and run it. Plain chit-chat can't be executed as an action.

exercises.prompt: You're building a loop for an AI that answers 'How many days until New Year?' It needs today's date. Put the loop in the right order.

options.text: Prompt the LLM → it outputs get_date() → code runs it and gets the date → feed the date back → LLM answers.
LLM instantly knows the date → answers → loop never runs.
Code runs get_date() first → guesses a prompt → ignores the result.
User must supply the date, action, and answer themselves.

exercises.explain: The winning order is the four-step loop: prompt the model, parse the action it chooses (get_date), execute it in code, then feed the real result back so the LLM can finish the answer. That cycle of prompt → action → execute → feed back is the heart of every agent.
